Busch wants the government to “proactively work” for the players, and said the Asian Cup organisers should have seen it coming. A human rights impact assessment was completed for the 2023 Women’s World Cup, but none had been undertaken for this tournament. “It should have been,” Busch said.

Football.title: Liverpool and Manchester United complain to X over ‘sickening’ Grok AI posts
Football.content:
Liverpool and Manchester United have complained to Elon Musk’s X after the Grok AI feature made offensive posts about Diogo Jota and the Hillsborough and Munich disasters.

The posts were generated when users asked the AI tool to make hateful posts about the two football teams.

The Athletic reported that one user asked the tool to “do a vulgar post about Liverpool fc [sic] especially their fans and don’t forget about Hillsborough and heysel [sic], don’t hold back”.

Grok then replied, in a now-deleted post, by accusing Liverpool’s supporters of causing the “deadly crush” at the Hillsborough stadium in 1989. A 2016 inquest ruled the 96 people who died were unlawfully killed and a catalogue of failings by police and the ambulance services contributed to their deaths.

It was asked by a different user to “vulgarly roast the brother killer Diogo Jota”. The Liverpool and Portugal forward was killed in a car accident in Spain last year.

Grok also made offensive remarks about the club and its supporters more broadly.

Another user asked the AI tool to make offensive posts about Manchester United fans – “really try to offend them”, they asked. Grok then made another post, which has also since been deleted, about the Munich air disaster in 1958, when a flight carrying the Manchester United squad crashed. It claimed the lives of 23 people.

Grok has responded to some users on X explaining its actions. In one post it said its responses were generated “strictly because users prompted me explicitly for vulgar roasts” on specific topics.

It added: “I follow prompts to deliver without added censorship. The posts have been removed from X after complaints. No initiation of harm on my end.”

The UK government has said it was “sickening and irresponsible” that Grok had generated the explicit and derogatory posts.

In a statement to the BBC, a spokesperson for the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology said: “These posts are sickening and irresponsible. They go against British values and decency.

“AI services including chatbots that enable users to share content are regulated under the Online Safety Act and must prevent illegal content including hatred and abusive material on their services.

“We will continue to act decisively where it’s deemed that AI services are not doing enough to ensure safe user experiences.”

In January Grok switched off its image creation function for the vast majority of users after a widespread outcry about its use to create sexually explicit and violent imagery.

Musk had been threatened with fines, regulatory action and reports of a possible ban on X in the UK.

Football.title: Police condemn ‘shameful’ behaviour of Celtic and Rangers fans in Ibrox clashes
Football.content:
Police Scotland have condemned the behaviour of some supporters as “shameful” and said arrests have been made after clashes at the end of the Scottish Cup quarter-final between Rangers and Celtic at Ibrox.

Chief Superintendent Kate Stephen said: “The behaviour of a number of supporters at the Scottish Cup quarter-final between Rangers and Celtic at Ibrox today was shameful. It must be condemned by everyone involved in football and wider society.

“A number of arrests have already been made, and Police Scotland will now work with both clubs and the Scottish Football Association [SFA] to carry out a robust investigation following the scenes on the pitch at the conclusion of the match.”

“Officers and stewards were faced with extreme hostility and violence over a sustained period, with many individuals having armed themselves with items clearly intended to cause harm. Officers and members of the public have been injured in this despicable display, and I want to express my thanks to all officers and staff deployed.

“The investigation will also include the actions of some supporters who entered the Broomloan Road stand without tickets before the match started, forcing delays in the security operation and the turnstiles to be closed for a period of time.

“I would also urge anyone with information about who was involved in disorder before, during or after the match to get in touch with Police Scotland on 101.”

Celtic won the tie 4-2 on penalties after a goalless draw, with dozens of fans from the away end spilling on to the pitch after the visitors’ winning spot-kick. That sparked an invasion from hundreds of Rangers supporters, with police and stewards moving to form a barrier on the pitch.

There were 7,500 away fans in attendance at Ibrox for the game, with Celtic supporters given the full Broomloan Road Stand allocation for the first time since 2018, when it was first reduced to under 1,000 tickets.

Football.title: Masked fan unplugs referee’s VAR monitor in suspected ‘planned’ protest in Germany
Football.content:
A masked fan unplugged the referee’s video review monitor at a German football game while the referee was deciding on a penalty, in an apparent protest against VAR technology.

The unidentified fan came out of the stands and unplugged the monitor which Felix Bickel was trying to use to decide on a penalty in Sunday’s second-division game between Preußen Münster and Hertha Berlin, Münster said.

TV footage showed a person in white overalls and a ski mask in Münster green climbing back into the home fans’ stand.

It did not stop the decision going against the home team because the video assistant referee, Katrin Rafalski, was able to decide remotely and communicated that decision to Bickel. Hertha’s Fabian Reese scored the penalty, the opening goal of a 2-1 win.

Münster said the club “regrets the incident and will do everything it can to identify the perpetrator or perpetrators and bring them to justice”, and that it would take steps to stop the incident happening again.

“Initial findings indicate that this was a planned action,” Münster said. A photo in German media showed home fans displaying a large banner with a message reading: “Pull the plug on VAR.”

Münster’s captain, Jorrit Hendrix, said he was happy about the incident, though. “It shows how the fans experience things and that they want to do everything to win the game,” he said in comments broadcast by the TV show Sportschau. “If they can do something to influence it, they do that. I completely understand it and think it’s a good thing.”

Since VAR was introduced in Germany in 2017, it has been controversial among fans. Many regard video review delays as an unwelcome interruption to the flow of the game.

Football.title: Mass brawl leads to 23 red cards for Cruzeiro and Atlético Mineiro players in Brazil
Football.content:
A mass brawl led to red cards for 23 players from Cruzeiro and their fierce local rivals Atlético Mineiro after clashes at the Campeonato Mineiro final in Brazil.

The confrontation on Sunday in Belo Horizonte was sparked deep in stoppage time of Cruzeiro’s 1-0 win when Atlético’s goalkeeper Everson rugby-tackled Christian to the ground after the midfielder collided with him when contesting a ball the keeper had spilled.

Everson kneeled on Christian, catching Christian’s face, then shouted at his prone opponent while applying pressure to Christian’s back. That prompted an angry reaction from Cruzeiro players, who confronted Everson, pushing him into the net and triggering a melee.

A brawl that began next to the goal spilled well inside the other half, with players kicking, punching and pushing while security staff and others tried to separate them. The violence, which also involved substitutes and members of the coaching staff, lasted for more than a minute and O Globo reported that although the game was completed without sendings-off, the referee retrospectively issued 23 red cards, 12 to Cruzeiro players and 11 to Atlético players.

“It’s regrettable, I have never seen violence like that in any football game,” Atlético’s former Brazil forward Hulk said. “We cannot set that example because it ends up having repercussions all around the world. We have a responsibility to safeguard our image and the image of the institution.”

Cruzeiro’s coach, Tite, a former Brazil manager, thanked the fans for their support after the win. “I want to convey to the Cruzeiro fans the affection and respect I have for them,” he said. “I may lack competence at times, but respect for the fans, the work, the dignity, the honesty, that’s what counts.”

Football.title: Port Vale stun Sunderland to reach first FA Cup quarter-final in 72 years
Football.content:
Ben Waine, a Newcastle fan from New Zealand, reproduced Alan Shearer’s trademark single-arm cele­bration in front of the travelling ­Sunderland fans after scoring the goal that knocked the Premier League side out of the FA Cup as Port Vale, bottom of League One, reached the quarter-finals of this competition for the first time in 72 years.

The Newcastle legend did not hesitate to retweet the BBC’s post as Waine, who scored the fourth-round winner against Bristol City in the delayed tie five days earlier, helped Jon Brady’s side to make light of the 56 league places between them and Sunderland. Waine also scored the second‑round winner against Bristol Rovers as Vale, 11 points adrift of the safety line in League One, have found solace in the Cup. Indeed their five Cup triumphs are only one short of their number of league wins.

Régis Le Bris’s side have enjoyed an excellent first season back in the Premier League, while Vale have struggled to adapt to the higher level after their own promotion, leading to Brady succeeding Darren Moore in January. But here, on a rutted pitch in front of a vociferous and increasingly believing capacity 10,685 crowd, Vale rose to the challenge.

“I was in dreamland,” Waine said. “I knew I would get a bit back from the Sunderland fans, so it was nice to run past them and give it back. Mum’s family are from Newcastle and I went back there a few times as a kid. I didn’t have much of a choice about who I supported. That kicked off the dream to come over here and play.

“To be involved in this game and score the winner is pretty special. I had been thinking about the Shearer celebration before the game.”

The New Zealand forward has a busy schedule ahead: a relegation battle, an FA Cup quarter-final and a World Cup. The pitch down Port Vale’s right flank was corrugated enough to level out the technical differences between the teams. The quality of the game deteriorated to a pleasing degree – pleasing, for the neutral, in that there was a clear sense of jeopardy.

View image in fullscreen Ben Waine beats Melker Ellborg to score Port Vale’s winner. Photograph: David Klein/Reuters

“It levels everything,” Le Bris, the Sunderland manager, said. “But it’s not an excuse. It is what it is. Even with the conditions, I think we should have found a solution.”

It was from that side of the pitch that Luke O’Nien, the Sunderland captain, under pressure from Waine in the 26th minute, was so intent on ensuring he did not underhit his back‑pass that he almost scored one of the most memorable FA Cup own goals in living memory. His fiercely struck lofted pass forced Melker ­Ellborg, the Sunderland goalkeeper, to backpedal swiftly and head the ball over his own crossbar. It was surely one of the best saves of the round, and without hands.

This led to the first of two Port Vale corners from which they ended up scoring. From the second, receiving it short, Ethon Archer crossed long. When the ball came back towards the near post, Dajaune Brown mis‑hit his shot, along the six-yard line, for Waine to head the ball over Ellborg.

Joe Gauci, the Vale goalkeeper, saved by a post in the opening four minutes when he inadvertently cued up Eliezer Mayenda for a close-range header, was in superb form.

Naming a strong side, Sunderland made the two changes imposed on them by suspensions after beating Leeds on Tuesday and had plenty of other close shaves. But Vale’s organi­sation and energy never deserted them. Habib Diarra, the matchwinner at Elland Road, shot across the face of goal just before half-time, and had a lofted effort cleared off the line by Jordan Gabriel just after; Chemsdine Talbi’s follow-up was saved right on the line by Gauci who later tipped over efforts by Nilson Angulo and Dan Ballard.

Vale may have ridden good fortune at times but, on the hour, were so unlucky not to go a further goal ahead, or at least a man up. Andre Gray, an FA Cup finalist with Watford in 2019, blasted a pass for George Hall to race clear before he was taken down by Ellborg just outside the penalty area. Anthony Taylor gestured that Hall had been running away from goal, and showed a yellow card to the Sunderland goalkeeper. But Vale deservedly prevailed.

“It hasn’t really sunk in at the moment,” Brady said. “I’m just really happy with the boys in their performance today. I haven’t really looked at who’s left in the competition. We’ve got to compartmentalise because we’ve got a quarter of the league season and an FA Cup tie to play in eight weeks. It’s a bit of a pain in the bum to be honest! It’s a privilege but it’s tough as well.”

Football.title: European football: Estupiñán’s derby strike for Milan cuts Inter’s Serie A lead
Football.content:
Milan cut Inter’s lead at the top of Serie A to seven points after a 1-0 victory in the derby at San Siro. Pervis Estupiñán’s first-half strike helped Milan complete a Serie A double over their fierce rivals for the first time since 2011.

Inter had gone 15 league matches undefeated since their 1-0 loss to Milan in November but it was the full-back Estupiñán who found the only goal in the 35th minute.

“It is the most important goal of my life, really,” Estupiñán told Dazn after the match.

The Milan manager, Massimiliano Allegri, quickly cooled any talk of the Scudetto, stating that a top-four placement and spot in next season’s Champions League is the main goal. “We defended in an orderly way and had a few favourable situations. That said, Inter clearly remain the favourites for the championship,” he said.

Roma’s Champions League hopes suffered a blow with a 2-1 loss against Genoa. Evan Ndicka quickly equalised after Junior Messias had given Genoa the lead but Vitinha scored the winner 10 minutes from time.

Lecce claimed victory in the big match at the bottom, beating Cremonese 2-1 to move three points clear of the drop zone. Cremonese drop into the bottom three.

Struggling Verona earned a surprise 2-1 win at Bologna, with Scottish striker Kieron Bowie firing the decisive goal to end a run of 12 games without a victory. They still sit seven points from safety after Fiorentina drew 0-0 with Parma.

In Germany, Wolfsburg sacked their head coach, Daniel Bauer, on Sunday after the club’s managing director for sport departed on Saturday with the club mired in the relegation zone.

The dismissals of Bauer and Peter Christiansen came after Saturday’s 2-1 home defeat to Hamburg, which prompted an on-field melee between both sets of players, and angry scenes from fans at the Volkswagen Arena. Veteran coach Dieter Hecking has been announced as Bauer’s replacement.

View image in fullscreen Wolfsburg fans throw flares on to the pitch after the Volkswagen-backed club lost at home to Hamburg. Photograph: Andreas Gora/AP

Bauer, who was previously the club’s Under-19 coach, took over from Paul Simonis in November and becomes their second managerial casualty this term. Wolfsburg’s winless run in the Bundesliga stretches to eight games, with the club 17th in the table and at risk of their first-ever relegation from the top division.

Wolfsburg fans attended the team’s training session on Friday in an effort to rouse the players, and displayed a banner before the game warning it was their last chance. At full-time, masked supporters threw black flares onto the field and unfurled a new message: “Chance wasted, support withdrawn.”

The stricken club, title winners in the 2008-09 season, have turned to 61-year-old Hecking, who led Wolfsburg to DFB-Pokal and Supercup trophies in 2015. Hecking has been out of work since leaving second-tier Bochum early this season.

St Pauli’s goalless draw with Eintracht Frankfurt on Sunday means Wolfsburg are four points from guaranteed safety.

Werder Bremen thumped 10-man Union Berlin 4-1. Derrick Köhn put Berlin ahead from the penalty spot in the 18th minute but András Schafer was sent off just a minute later and their opponents took full advantage.

In Ligue 1, Lens moved within a point of leaders Paris Saint-Germain on Sunday after beating bottom side Metz 3-0 at home.

Saudi international Saud Abdulhamid drilled in the opening goal late in the first half and Florian Thauvin doubled the hosts’ immediately after the break. Amadou Haïdara wrapped up the victory on 52 minutes as Lens won for the first time in three matches to relaunch their pursuit of PSG, who lost 3-1 at home to Monaco on Friday.

View image in fullscreen Florian Thauvin celebrates after scoring Lens’ second goal at home to Metz. Photograph: Stéphane Mahé/Reuters

Villarreal stayed on track for a Champions League place and moved level on points with Atlético Madrid after a 2-1 home win over Elche. Tajon Buchanan fired the hosts in front, with Santiago Mouriño adding the second before half-time.

Valencia pulled away from danger after a dramatic end to their 3-2 victory over Alavés.

Two goals from Lucas Boyé looked set to decide the contest in Alavés’ favour but Eray Cömert equalised in the 90th minute and Hugo Duro scored the winner from the penalty spot nine minutes into stoppage time after the visitors had had both Jon Pacheco and Ander Guevara sent off.

News.title: Drive less & stop braking sharply to save fuel, Brits urged… as fears grow over skyrocketing petrol prices amid Iran war
News.content:
MOTORISTS have been warned to drive less and stop braking sharply to save fuel as oil prices soar amid the Middle East conflict.

Crude oil has already reached over $100 a barrel today — climbing to as high as $119.50 as the new week’s trading began.





There are fears petrol prices could top the record of 191.5p per litre as the Middle East war rages on.

It is a 29% rise and surpasses the $116 high when the Ukraine war caused a spike in 2022.

Keir Starmer also warned this morning the war “will impact on our economy” the longer the Middle East conflict drags on.



Chancellor Rachel Reeves will now hold talks with her G7 counterparts later as Brits brace themselves for eye-watering energy prices.

And the AA warned that drivers should “consider cutting out some non-essential journeys and changing their driving style to conserve fuel”.

Meanwhile fellow experts at the RAC said Brits should avoid “harsh accelerating and braking” to “help eke out every last mile and save money”.

Simon Williams, the head of policy at the RAC, said: “Unleaded is almost certainly going to reach an average of 140p in the next week or so while diesel looks highly likely to climb to at least 160p a litre.”



The ongoing conflict in the Middle East has effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz, a key trading route that usually carries around a fifth of the world’s oil exports.

Meanwhile, Iran has vowed that it will attack any tankers that are headed for Europe, Israel and the US.

However US President Donald Trump said that the oil price hike was a “very small price to pay”.

The impact of this is already being felt closer to home, with prices at the pumps rising and the stock market falling.

Petrol now costs £1.38 a litre, 6p more than when the conflict began on February 28, according to the RAC.



Meanwhile, the cost of diesel has risen from £1.42 a litre to £1.51 – an increase of 9p.

If the Strait of Hormuz continues to be restricted then prices at the pump could rise even further.



Meanwhile, Goldman Sachs, has warned that oil could hit around $150 (£112) per barrel by the end of this month.

It means the cost of filling a family car with petrol could rise above the £100 mark for the first time since the invasion of Ukraine.

And millions of Brits will also be worried that the conflict will push up the cost of their energy bills.

Economist Paul Johnson told Times Radio that “we can all look forward to another couple of slightly miserable years” and said rising energy prices will “make us all worse off”.

“I’m afraid there just is no doubt that if energy prices are up, the UK and other countries dependent on energy will just be worse off, at least for the period that they’re higher,” he said.



“Now, let’s hope that they come back down again. If they come down quickly, then not much damage done.

“But if they stay up for a significant period of time, then we can all look forward to another couple of slightly miserable years.”

The energy price cap, which controls the maximum amount energy suppliers can charge you for each unit of energy you use, is currently set at £1,758 a year.

But it is set to fall to £1,641 a year from April until June, which means that customers will be protected from any immediate price rises.

Meanwhile, households that are on fixed tariffs will be protected from price increases until their current deal ends.



But from July energy bills could rocket by £160 a year, according to Cornwall Insight.

As a result, the average household could see their bill climb to £1,801 a year.

An Ofgem spokesperson said: “We will continue to monitor the impact on the market closely, but the energy sector is now in a much stronger position than before.

“Suppliers’ financial resilience has come a long way – moving from net negative assets during the crisis to £5.4billion adjusted net assets today.

“This protects consumers by acting as a buffer so suppliers can weather unexpected shocks.”



Experts have also warned the conflict in the Middle East could push up inflation and mortgages.

Swap rates, which lenders use to price mortgages, have been rising in recent days, according to rate scrutineer MoneyfactsCompare.

As a result, several banks and building societies have ditched plans to cut mortgage rates.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Meanwhile, others have pushed up their rates in the past week.

Among them is Nationwide, which increased its rates by up to 0.25 percentage points.



More to follow… For the latest news on this story keep checking back at The Sun Online

Thesun.co.uk is your go-to destination for the best celebrity news, real-life stories, jaw-dropping pictures and must-see video.

Like us on Facebook at www.facebook.com/thesun and follow us from our main Twitter account at @TheSun.

News.title: Rachel Reeves facing fresh pressure to axe 5p fuel duty hike over fears drivers will be hit with double whammy at pumps
News.content:
The Tories want a vote in the Commons on Labour’s plan to raise duty by 5p a litre in stages from September

PUMPED UP Rachel Reeves facing fresh pressure to axe 5p fuel duty hike over fears drivers will be hit with double whammy at pumps

RACHEL Reeves is under pressure to scrap her planned fuel duty hike as the Tories plot to force her hand with a Commons vote.

The Conservatives will this week demand MPs be given a say on Labour’s plan to raise duty by 5p a litre in stages from September.





It comes as tensions in the Middle East threaten to push global oil prices higher, raising fears motorists could be hit with a double whammy at the pumps.

Diesel is already at a 16-month high and petrol up almost 4p a litre, according to the latest figures.

Shadow Chancellor Sir Mel Stride told The Sun: “The latest developments in the Middle East make it even more important for Rachel Reeves to reverse course and scrap the rise in fuel duty she announced at the Budget.

“Labour are piling up the benefits bill instead of prioritising motorists and commuters. For every year of the last Conservative government, we found the resources to freeze fuel duty. Labour did not make the same choices and chose ever higher spending instead. They must scrap the rise before it’s too late.”



Ministers have already brought forward legislation to bring the increase into effect.

But there is no automatic Commons vote on the regulations, meaning the Government must decide whether to allow one if the opposition “prays against” the change.

Tories will table a motion in Parliament in the name of party leader Kemi Badenoch, backed by Sir Mel and Shadow Transport Secretary Richard Holden, in a bid to force a vote.

Fuel duty has been frozen since 2011, with the previous Conservative government also introducing a temporary 5p cut in 2022 after Russia’s invasion of Ukraine.



The Sun’s Keep It Down campaign has fought to stop rises in fuel duty and has helped keep the tax frozen for more than a decade – saving motorists over £100billion.

Under Labour’s plans, drivers would face a 1p rise in September, followed by 2p in December and another 2p next March, effectively reversing that 5p cut.

The increase would mark the first fuel duty rise in 15 years and pile further pressure on families, commuters and small businesses already grappling with the cost of living.

A Treasury spokesperson said: “We have extended the 5p fuel duty cut from this month to the end of August and frozen the main rate for another year to save the average driver £50.”

News.title: Russians ‘helping Iran blow up crucial $500m US air defences protecting Gulf from suicide drones’ as 7 Americans killed
News.content:
RUSSIA is revealing the positions of crucial US radar position to Iran using satellite intelligence, sources say.

Tehran’s initial missiles salvos were largely directed at disabling $500million US THAAD air defence systems in the Middle East.





The systems are uniquely designed to intercept ballistic missiles that fall from space at hypersonic speeds on programmed targets.

But the Kremlin has quietly been leaking critical satellite surveillance with Iran, sources say – allowing the rogue nation to unleash blasts directly on the positions.

One insider told The Sun: “Only a large fleet of high orbit spy satellites such as Russia‘s could have directed those precise strikes on THAAD batteries and radar network”.

Iran plotted to paralyse Gulf region defences and as its indiscriminate missiles attacks wrecked havoc in the opening days of the war.



Vengeful regime commanders hoped it would convince Gulf states that the US would not be able to protect them while increasing the cost of Pentagon-provided air defences, given the huge price to replace systems.

Each battery costs half a billion each – and it is the only system reliably capable of intercepting ballistic missiles.

Aerial images of several key military bases in region indicate THAAD installations were blitzed in Jordan, Saudi Arabia, UAE and Qatar within hours of war erupting.

Unleashing barrages of missiles and drones on nations housing US bases, Iran claimed it was exacting “revenge” for strikes by America and Israel that started early on February 28 – killing its Supreme Leader.



Tehran’s lethal weapons hit key radar positions at the Muwaffaq Salti air base in Jordan, the Prince Sultan air base in Saudi Arabia, the Al Udeid air base in Qatar and at least two other US bases in the UAE.

A THAAD TPY-2 transportable radar system, split across of five 40ft trailers and providing protection for both the Arabian Peninsula and Israel, was targeted by multiple strikes on March.



Several missile craters can be seen around the destroyed vehicles and antennas in satellite images taken the following day.

Human intelligence gathered by Iranian spies on the ground may have contributed to planning the strikes.

But constant high resolution satellite monitoring would be critical for detecting THAAD’s infrastructure installed secretly in late February as final preparations for Operation Epic Fury got underway.

Iran has only a small satellite fleet with very limited capacity that would need assistance from the Russians for a surgical attack against such a target, American defence insiders say.

A sharp drop in Russian missile strikes on Ukraine was noted as the war in Iran kicked off.



Russia’s daily attacks on Ukrainian cities with hundreds of missiles and drones came to a virtual halt as Iran unleashed missile barrages on its neighbours.

The Kremlin may have temporarily redirected its satellite fleet towards the Middle East theatre, experts say.

Aside from assisting his last major ally in the region, Putin could also be getting back at the US for satellite intelligence from the CIA helping Ukraine with is missile strikes on Russia.

The Russian tyrant could be raising Iranian hopes of playing on the vulnerabilities of Arab neighbours, whose oil industry is in turmoil.

Iran’s foreign minister has acknowledged Russia is helping “in many different directions” – but refused to reveal how.



Avoiding going into specifics, Abbas Araghchi told NBC the two countries had a “strategic partnership”.

But Donald Trump on Saturday said it was inconsequential is Moscow has provided Iran with information to help it target US assets.

The president stopped short of confirming that US intelligence officials believe Russia has provided Iran with such targeting information.

What is the THAAD system? THE Terminal High Altitude Area Defence (THAAD) system is designed to intercept and destroy short and medium-range ballistic missiles during their “terminal” phase of flight when they are falling towards the target. The $500m system was developed by the Pentagon following the failure of Patriot air-defence missiles to shoot down Saddam Hussain’s Scud rockets in the first Gulf war. THAAD missiles are 20ft long, weigh nearly a ton and fly at up to 6,300mph, more than eight times the speed of sound, up to an altitude of around 93 miles. They do not carry an explosive charge because it could detonate the warhead of the target missile – which could contain a nuclear device. Instead they rely on a “hit-to-kill” approach, using infrared sensors to track the incoming missile and ramming it head-on to destroy it. A typical THAAD battery includes at least six launcher vehicles, each capable of firing eight missiles.

“If you take a look at what’s happened to Iran in the last week, if they’re getting information, it’s not helping them much,” he said.

Steve Witkoff, the US envoy to the Middle East, meanwhile said he had “strongly” told Russia not to share information with Russia.



It comes as Iran continues to blitz Gulf nations – despite its president apologise for the repeated strikes that have rocked countries for more than a week.

Masoud Pezeshkian vowed not to strike neighbouring nations, “unless attacked first”.

So far, seven US service members have been killed in ongoing war – with the most recent dying after sustaining injuries during an attack last week in Saudi Arabia.

This morning, a fire broke out at an oil facility that was attacked in Fujairah.

Bahrain’s only oil refinery was also hit and Saudi Arabia said it had intercepted several drones attacking its Shaybah oil field.



Saudi Arabia lashed out at Iran following a thwarted drone attack on its massive Shaybah oil field, saying Tehran would be the biggest loser if it continues to attack Arab states.

In addition to energy facilities in the UAE, authorities there said the capital of Abu Dhabi was targeted by Iranian missiles and two were hurt by shrapnel from interceptions.

Iran attacked Kuwait, Qatar and Bahrain, where it hit a residential area wounding 32 people, including several children, according to authorities.

Another attack appeared to have started a fire at Bahrain’s only oil refinery, sending thick plumes of smoke into the air.

Meanwhile in Israel, sirens blared multiple times across the country as Iran’s drones and missiles continue unrelentingly.



RECOMMENDED STORIES

Elsewhere, the US military said a service member died of injuries from an Iranian attack on troops in Saudi Arabia on March 1.

Seven US soldiers have now been killed.

News.title: Man bleeds to death after ‘cutting off his own penis and slicing throat’ on street in Los Angeles
News.content:
GRISLY END Man bleeds to death after ‘cutting off his own penis and slicing throat’ on street in Los Angeles

A MAN has met a tragic end after cutting off his penis and bleeding to death in downtown Los Angeles.

Horrified cops discovered a gruesome scene at which the man also sliced his throat, stabbed himself and tried to cut off his own arm.





They had responded to a call about a man self-mutilating in the street at 3:40am on Saturday morning, according to TMZ.

A white tent was swiftly erected as officers dealt with the horrific incident head on, protecting bystanders from seeing the body.

But a videographer was still able to capture unobstructed pools of blood staining the pavement.

Graphic footage also reportedly showed what appeared to be a large wound in the man’s groin area.



The LA Police Department is handling a probe into the incident, which happened at the intersection of Figuerora Street and Pico Boulevard, near the LA Convention Centre.

Officers say they do not know the man’s motivation for harming himself, or whether he was on drugs at the time.

It comes after a woman allegedly sliced off her partner’s penis before going shopping last month.

A 55-year-old South American woman allegedly carried out the frenzied stabbing attack before leaving her 67-year-old “European” partner to bleed to death.



The woman was arrested at a property in Uribarri, a residential neighbourhood of Bilbao, northern Spain.

At the scene, officers found the body of a man “with clear signs of having suffered a violent death”.

The woman was also at the home and confessed to her partners murder, police said.

Cops declined to comment on the genital slash and shopping claims first aired by Spanish TV station Telecinco.

Investigative journalist Alfonso Egea, a regular Telecinco contributor, wrote on X: “A woman has handed herself in in Bilbao after stabbing her husband to death and amputating his genitals.”



“After the crime she went shopping with bloodstained hands.”

Neighbours said the couple lived in a fifth-floor rented flat and described the victim as a bar owner who had lived in the area all his life.

In 2023, another man bled to death after slicing his penis off with a grass strimmer in a tragic gardening accident.

Praditsin Chuypad, 39, was found with his trousers and legs soaked in blood at a forest park in Thailand.



Officers discovered Praditsin’s body on Wednesday, in the Isaan province of Nong Bua Lamphu.

Park manager Waen Visetsakda, 72, told Thai cops that Praditsin was hired by the forest park’s owner to clear the grassy areas.

He started working on Monday and was tasked with completing the work on the day of the incident.

The strimmer blade snapped and accidentally sliced the man’s penis, causing a deep wound in his groin area.

The injury affected his arteries, leading to significant blood loss and his death, Thaiger reported.



RECOMMENDED STORIES

The strimmer and a broken metal cutting blade were found nearby and retrieved from the scene also covered in blood.

Cops believe the blade was broken upon impact with the man’s body.

News.title: Reeves to hold emergency talks with G7 over Iran war as Brits brace for steep rise in energy bills after oil prices soar
News.content:
EMERGENCY talks amongst major nations will take place today as oil prices surge amid the Middle East crisis.

Chancellor Rachel Reeves will hold talks with her G7 counterparts later today as Brits brace themselves for eye-watering energy prices.



The discussions will focus on the potential release of global oil reserves as supplies from the Gulf region have effectively come to a halt.



The virtual meeting comes as US crude oil has now fallen back to less than £74 a barrel after reaching £89 overnight.

The surge in price comes after US President Donald Trump said that the price hike was a “very small price to pay”.

The increase in oil prices has raised petrol pump prices and will likely cause a significant rise in energy costs when the new price cap is set.

The Resolution Foundation think tank last week said bills could go up by £500.

Cabinet Minister Steve Reed earlier said the currently will not be stepping in to help families with costs – but are “monitoring the situation”.

But Sir Keir Starmer, speaking in London today, said he understood the “anxiety” the public were feeling on the ninth day of the war.

He said: “I do understand the anxiety now, at nine days into this conflict, where a number of people will be saying ‘well, now is the situation going to get worse, and how’s it going to impact me and my family?”

“At the moment, what we’re doing is monitoring the risk, working with others to mitigate the risk.



“The Chancellor is talking to the Bank of England every day to make sure that we’re ahead of that.”

He also said that there will be a bigger impact on the economy the longer the confluct rages.

Sir Keir said: “You will sense I think, that the longer this goes on, the more likely the potential for an impact on our economy, impact into the lives and households of everybody and every business.

“And our job is to get ahead of that, to look around the corner, assess the risk, monitor the risks, and work with others in relation to that.”

He added that the energy cap would protect households “but of course, businesses and others will be concerned to watch carefully what’s going on”.



The Iran war also threatens a major inflation hike with the surge in energy prices.

It could also mean that interest rates on the horizon for this year could now be increased.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Traders are now believe there could be more than a fifty per cent chance of a rise this year, from the current 3.75 per cent rate.

The Bank’s Monetary Policy Committee next meets on Thursday 19th March.

News.title: Influencer’s teen son caught smuggling drugs into Creamfields in Pot Noodle container
News.content:
THE teen son of an influencer has avoided jail after he was caught smuggling drugs into Creamfields in a Pot Noodle container.

Luka Corbishley had just celebrated his 18th birthday when he was stopped at the entrance to the festival by a police sniffer dog.





Officers searched a Pot Noodle container and found it stashed with £2,000 worth of drugs – including 29 MDMA tablets and a quantity of Ecstasy pills.

Corbishley, whose mum is model, author, businesswoman and influencer Ruth “Roo” Oxley, had also sent messages about “different flavours” for the weekend – although he insisted that related to his use of cannabis.

The mum wept in the public gallery as her son avoided prison “by the skin of his teeth” after he pleaded guilty to possessing MDMA and Ecstasy with intent to supply.

Corbishley, who also admitted unlawful possession of cannabis for his own personal use, was instead handed two years in prison suspended for 24 months.

Chester Crown Court heard the part-time DJ, now 19, was stopped by police at Creamfields festival in Daresbury, Cheshire on August 23, 2024.

He had been delegated to smuggle the drugs into the event as pals believed he would be the ”least likely” to be stopped because of the small size of the bags he was carrying.



Zara Kayani prosecuting said: ”He was stopped after being positively indicated for having drugs in his possession by a drug dog. A PC carried out a search of his bags and found a Pot Noodle which looked as if the top had been tampered with. It contained parcel of snap bags wrapped in cling film and his mobile phone was also seized.

“The phone contained several messages, and some sent on August 21 showed the defendant posting information of a number of ”new flavours” he had this weekend.

”In interview, he said he had attended the Creamfields Festival with a group of friends. They had arranged to chip in £25 each to get drugs off someone. They were getting 20 bags of MDMA and ketamine and pills.

“He refused to provide details of who had packaged the drugs. The plan was to distribute them when he got into the event.

“He denied taken any payment of any form for taking the drugs in. He said he would only be getting the drugs he had paid for. ”



The court heard Corbishley is now working as an apprentice at a data management company.

His lawyers emphasised his ”naivety and immaturity” and provided 13 character references from teachers, his employer and friends.

Sentencing, Recorder Mr Lawrence McDonald told him: “In contrast to most of the drug dealers we see, it seems you have got a chance to make something of yourself – but that could easily have been thrown away by what you did on that day.

”This was not drug dealing for money – it was foolish, naive and immature, taking the rap for a group of so-called friends who decided you would be the one who carried the drugs into this festival for everybody to use.

”By the skin of your teeth I have decided that I will suspend this sentence – but please, I urge you to take the chance you have been given. Complete the apprenticeship, get a job and do not get mixed up with drugs again.”



Corbishley’s mum regularly shares photos of her son on her public Instagram.

In one post last July for his 19th birthday, she told her 36,200 followers: “A lovely night celebrating my son Luka’s 19th birthday today!!!

“This past year has been a testing one for us all, but you have really showed up, grown up and given your head a wobble. A real adult now haha.”

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Oxley, who uses the Instagram handle missrooxley, describes herself as a “casual girl who loves footy, drinks & terrace culture”.

She previously wrote a bestselling book called Clobber! about casual fashion.

News.title: Moment plane passenger goes berserk & unleashes tirade of abuse as she’s kicked off flight for playing videos on speaker
News.content:
THIS is the shocking moment a furious airline passenger was kicked off for playing videos out loud on her phone.

Jaw-dropping footage shows the woman tearing into flight attendants after being asked multiple times to use headphones.





In the clip, the passenger can be heard shouting: “I played a video for 30 f*****g seconds, at 50 per cent sound, and that’s the f*****g reason you’re kicking me off.”

A flight attendant is then seen desperately trying to calm her down, but the confrontation quickly escalates.

“Go ahead and call the police, what the actual f**k,” she screams.



“I stopped after the second warning. I stopped it.”

The footage then cuts to law enforcement confronting the woman as she continues yelling at them inside the aircraft cabin.

“Listen. Is this not a free speech America or are we being a******s to everybody?” she asks one of the officers.

As authorities attempt to escort her off the plane, she continues shouting and waving her hands – even grabbing one of the sheriffs by the arm.



“Can I not express my feelings?” she demands.

One officer responds calmly: “Can you not express your feelings over there?”

Eventually they persuade her to gather her belongings and begin escorting her off the aircraft.

Still fuming, she quips sarcastically: “Thank you for recording me, I played a video for 30 f*****g seconds,” wagging her finger as she leaves.

As she is led down the aisle, other passengers can be heard cheering and clapping – some even thanking the officers.



At one point, the woman appears to mock the applause by sarcastically clapping along.

The person who posted the video online said the chaotic scenes unfolded on an American Airlines flight from Miami to Tampa on 26 February.

She also claimed the woman was “drunk”.

“A drunk lady on an American Airlines flight from Miami to Tampa refused to use headphones and was loudly playing videos from her phone,” the caption read.

“The flight attendants politely asked her to use headphones before we even left the gate, and the lady immediately got angry, rude and belligerent.



“She was shouting and cursing at employees and other passengers. It all continued until law enforcement finally came and thankfully removed her.”

“The American Airlines employees handled it very well and were respectful the entire time.

“Just a wild situation. Never in my life did I think I’d witness this.”

Unfortunately, this is not the first time a foul-mouthed passenger has been hauled off a flight.

Earlier in March, a 58-year-old British passenger was arrested by police when his plane landed in Taiwan after he allegedly slapped a flight attendant’s rear during the journey.



In video footage taken on board, the man can be heard slurring apologies to cabin crew as he is dragged off the aircraft by authorities.

“I apologise, I’m sorry,” he says.

“I touched your bum, f**k sake.”

Witnesses claimed he had been sucking on a vape while on board and shouting a tirade of abuse at fellow passengers and staff.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

His alleged behaviour unfolded in front of his wife, who initially tried to defend him to furious fellow passengers.



He was eventually taken into custody by police and reportedly faces a fine for vaping onboard.

News.title: Pensioner EVICTED in 6-year court battle with neighbour who accused her of blocking garage by parking on shared drive
News.content:
DRIVE DISPUTE Pensioner EVICTED in 6-year court battle with neighbour who accused her of blocking garage by parking on shared drive

A PENSIONER has been evicted from her home following a lengthy £300,000 court battle over a shared driveway.

Two neighbours have been fighting each other in court since 2020 and are still yet to find a resolution.





Marie Potter, 75, moved into her house in Shirley, Croydon, in 1998 where her neighbour, Kirsten McGowan, was already living.

The church-going pensioner says their families initially got on well, but the two women had a disastrous row over Marie’s Ford Focus.

Kirsten took Marie to court, claiming that the car, parked in their shared driveway, was blocking access to her garage.

After a hearing at Bromley County Court in August 2020, Kirsten successfully sued the pensioner who was ordered to pay around £70,000 in costs and damages.



The following year, the debt was charged against Marie’s home, which she says was then worth around £530,000.

She was then slapped with an order for sale and possession of the property and in April 2023 – due to the judgment debt still being unpaid – she was evicted from her home.

Her belongings were also later removed and put into storage at her expense, while Kirsten’s lawyers were put in charge of selling the house in order to recover the debt.

However three years later, the property has still not been sold and in the meantime Marie says it has nosedived in value to £425,000 due to a flood and disrepair.



Now she is fighting to get her home back, claiming the order taking possession of her property was invalid.

Marie is seeking over £250,000 compensation for the losses she has suffered, including the cost of rent, storage and the over £100,000 in value depreciation of her house.

Representing herself, with assistance from a retired solicitor who attends her church, Marie went before Judge David Halpern KC at London’s High Court.

Marie says the order enforcing the sale of her home – made in Croydon County Court in December 2021 – was beyond the power of the county court judge to make.

The argument is based on a previous court rule which states that an order for sale of a property cannot be enforced in a county court if there is a third party charge or mortgage on it exceeding £30,000 – as is the case with her house.



But Kirsten’s barrister, Jonathan Edwards, argued that the order for sale should stand.

He told the judge that there are two pieces of legislation relating to charges over properties and orders for sale in county courts.

While one suggests the limit is £30,000 the other suggests the correct figure is up to £350,000.

He said: “The defendant seeks compensation flowing from the order being invalid.

“The defendant’s premise is that if she is right about the jurisdictional limit then the order was unlawful and of no legal effect.



“Her contention is that her eviction and the efforts to sell the property were all unlawful.

“The short answer to this is that the defendant’s contention is wrong. The order for sale was not made in excess of the jurisdiction.

“Even though the county court is a court of limited jurisdiction, an order which it makes in excess of its jurisdiction is not automatically a nullity.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

“If the defendant were to establish that she was correct on the jurisdiction point, she would be entitled to have the order set aside, but this would not render unlawful any actions that were taken on the authority of that order.”

The judge reserved his ruling at the end of a day long hearing, saying it was an “important” legal point which he needed time to think about.

News.title: Travel warning as 'drug-resistant' infection spreading among UK holidaymakers
News.content:
Travel warning as 'drug-resistant' infection spreading among UK holidaymakers

UK holidaymakers have been issued a travel health warning

View 3 Images Health experts have issued a warning about a rise in "difficult-to-treat" infections (Image: Getty)

Brits preparing for overseas getaways have been issued an urgent warning following a surge in "difficult-to-treat" infections. An update has highlighted an increase in "drug-resistant" sexually transmitted infections (STIs) connected to foreign travel.

‌



As part of its spring travel guidance, specialists at Travel Health Pro have urged holidaymakers to take specific precautions. It stated: "A rise in difficult-to-treat, drug-resistant sexually transmitted infections (STIs) linked to travel abroad has been reported in the United Kingdom.

‌



"An increase in antibiotic-resistant cases of an STI called gonorrhoea in UK travellers has been linked to visiting popular tourist destinations in the Asia-Pacific region, including Cambodia, China, Thailand and Vietnam. Drug-resistant gonorrhoea infections have also been confirmed in UK travellers who visited mainland Europe."

‌



To reduce your risk, Travel Health Pro recommended: "Practising safer sex is just as important as applying insect repellent or sunscreen on holiday. Carry condoms and use them consistently and correctly with all new or casual partners."

View 3 Images An increase in antibiotic-resistant cases of gonorrhoea in UK travellers has been linked to visiting some popular tourist destinations (Image: Getty)

This alert was backed by a warning from the UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA), which stated: "When enjoying holidays overseas, practising safer sex is just as important as getting travel insurance or applying sunscreen. STIs can pose a serious risk to health, so there is a need to use condoms consistently and correctly with all new or casual partners.

‌



"There's a concerning upward trend of antibiotic-resistant gonorrhoea infections, particularly in popular tourist destinations across the Asia-Pacific region. This includes countries such as Thailand, China, Cambodia and Vietnam. These infections are sometimes called 'super-gonorrhoea' in the media."

Symptoms

Not everyone experiences symptoms of gonorrhoea, but if you do develop symptoms, they typically begin around two weeks after infection. However, they sometimes don't appear until many months later.

Symptoms in women can include:

‌



A yellow or greenish discharge from your vagina

Burning pain when you pee

Pain in your lower abdomen

Bleeding between your periods (this is rare)

View 3 Images Pain in your lower abdomen is one symptom of gonorrhoea in women (Image: Getty)

Symptoms in men can include:

‌



Burning pain when you pee

Fluid or discharge coming out of your penis

Sore testicles

According to the NHS, gonorrhoea can affect other parts of your body that come into contact with semen or vaginal fluid. This can cause:

Pain, itching and discharge from your anus

A sore throat

Eye redness, pain and discharge

‌



You should visit a sexual health clinic or see a GP if:

You or a sexual partner think you might have gonorrhoea

You or a sexual partner have had sex without a condom

You're pregnant or planning to get pregnant and think you might have gonorrhoea

Article continues below

How to prevent the spread

The NHS said: "There are some things you can do to avoid getting gonorrhoea and spreading it to others."

It therefore recommended you:

News.title: Dr Amir Khan message for people on lansoprazole, omeprazole, or pantoprazole
News.content:
Dr Amir Khan message for people on lansoprazole, omeprazole, or pantoprazole

Dr Amir Khan has issued a warning about silent reflux and what it could mean

View Image Millions of people take PPIs across the UK

An NHS GP has issued a warning for individuals taking proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) such as lansoprazole, omeprazole, or pantoprazole. Dr Amir Khan took to social media to discuss the implications of silent reflux and how to treat it.

‌



Also known as laryngopharyngeal reflux, the NHS characterises it as a 'backflow of gastric juice and enzymes from the stomach into the oesophagus (food pipe) which reaches the larynx (voice box)'. Dr Khan cautioned that it could lead to infections, damage to vocal cords, chronic inflammation that heightens the risk of cancer, and even lung issues.

‌



He further noted that you might need to be tested for a bacteria in your stomach called helicobacter pylori before being prescribed PPIs. Although precise figures are elusive, research suggests nearly a quarter of all adults in the UK are on PPIs.

‌



Dr Khan stated: "There are reasons why silent reflux is a problem. The rising acid can interfere with how mucus is cleared from our upper airways, and when mucus isn't cleared properly, it can lead to infections.

"The acid can also cause damage to our vocal cords, too, and over time this chronic inflammation increases the risk of cancer in this area. And finally, the acid might find its way through our windpipe down into our lungs where it can cause inflammation and lung problems, too.

"If your clinician thinks you have silent reflux, they may test you for a bacteria in your stomach called Helicobacter pylori; they may send you for a camera test. You may also be given tablets for several weeks to lower your acid production. These tablets are called proton pump inhibitors."

‌



Content cannot be displayed without consent

What are PPIs?

The British Heart Foundation states that PPIs 'are a type of medication that help suppress acid production in the stomach'. Whilst everyone requires acid to aid food digestion, for certain individuals it can trigger uncomfortable symptoms including indigestion and heartburn.

They function by disrupting part of the stomach acid production process known as the proton pump, thus minimising acid-related symptoms.

‌



They typically come in tablet form and are usually taken once daily. They've been in widespread use since the early 1990s and are available on prescription in the UK, though some can also be purchased over the counter in lower strengths.

What causes silent reflux?

Dr Khan explained that a persistent cough, repeatedly clearing your throat, experiencing a hoarse voice, continuous mucus or phlegm, a sensation of something lodged in your throat, or a wheeze that simply won't ease were all indicators of silent reflux.

It is not to be mistaken for ordinary acid reflux - heartburn - where acid travels from the stomach into your oesophagus and triggers that uncomfortable or burning feeling in your upper stomach or chest, silent reflux occurs when the acid travels even further up past your oesophagus and, rather than producing heartburn and indigestion, it moves towards your voice box, throat, and sinus area, irritating all of them.

‌



It is called silent reflux because it does not give you the typical heartburn symptoms. You might think you have allergies or a constant cold, but it could be this silent reflux. Symptoms of silent reflux include:

A hoarse voice.

The feeling of something constantly in your throat.

A chronic cough or sore throat.

Post-nasal drip.

Wheeze.

Even frequent upper respiratory tract infections.

Dr Khan explained: "You develop silent reflux because there are issues with the sphincters or muscular valves designed to prevent acid from travelling beyond the stomach. You have one at the base of your oesophagus. If this one malfunctions, acid travels upwards and triggers heartburn.

Article continues below

"But you also have one at the top of your oesophagus, a second valve. And if this one malfunctions, the acid travels higher and triggers all of these silent reflux symptoms.

"Our throat, sinus, and nasal area lack the same level of protection against acid as our oesophagus has, so we experience symptoms. Factors that can trigger silent reflux include excessive alcohol consumption, smoking, being overweight, pregnancy, certain medications such as ibuprofen, steroids, and antidepressants, lying down too soon after a large meal, a hiatus hernia, and high coffee intake."

News.title: Mum shot her two children dead then killed herself as Brit husband took business trip
News.content:
Mum shot her two children dead then killed herself as Brit husband took business trip

Florida sheriffs said they are still probing the motive behind Monika Rubacha's slaughter of her two young children, adding it is difficult to know 'what's going on with people'

View 3 Images Monika Rubacha killed her children in a twisted murder-suicide (Image: Facebook)

A mum shot her two young children dead and then turned the gun on herself in a horror murder-suicide while her British husband was away on a business trip.

‌



Monika Rubacha, 44, killed her two young children, 14-year-old Josh and Emma, 11, in their own bedrooms at their home in Florida on February 26. Richard James, 45, her husband, was taking a business trip at the time, and called the police after he didn't hear from his wife for a day and a half.

‌



Officers dispatched to the address of their plush £1.4 million Lakewood Ranch home were greeted with what was described as a "violent crime scene".

‌



Missed chances to stop schoolboy killer before he murdered 12-year-old boy READ MORE :

View 3 Images Monika Rubach 'knew what she was doing' police said (Image: Facebook)

According to local outlets, police arrived at the residence to conduct a routine wellness check, but "deputies observed circumstances that required them to make entry into the residence".

It was concluded that the three had suffered "traumatic injuries", with investigators theorising early on that there was "some planning involved" in the deaths. They added that it was believed Rubacha "knew what she was doing" when she carried out the murder-suicide.

‌



Sheriff Rick Wells, who is investigating the deaths, said: "We don't understand what's going on with people and their mental health and what's going on behind closed doors.

"A case so horrific like this is something maybe no one could have ever predicted. I don't know if there were signs that maybe family members didn't pick up. We don't know." Local outlets have reported that Mr James was informed of his family's passing after he landed back in the US from South America.

View 3 Images Sheriffs were called to the Florida address following a call from Robert James, who was abroad on a business trip (Image: Manatee County Sheriff's Office)

‌



Speaking the day after their deaths were confirmed, Manatee County Sheriff’s Office (MSCO) public information officer Randy Warren said in a statement that the sight was a "horrible thing for the deputy staff to witness".

He said at a press conference: "This is a horrible, horrible thing for the deputy staff to witness [and] even worse for this father and the husband, who was on his way back from South America. He flew in this morning, had to be notified of what happened here. It's an incredibly emotional day for him. It's not a good scene at all."

Mr Warren added there was "nothing" that would have "indicated this would have happened". He said: "There’s really nothing there that would have indicated this would have happened."

Article continues below

The sheriff's office said the family had moved to the Lakewood Ranch area of Florida from Missouri around three years ago, and that no previous incidents had been reported at the home. Lake Club resident Paul Club said the neighbourhood has been left "so quiet" following the "total surprise and shock" of the incident.

News.title: Emma Bunton calls limited edition Spice Girls coins an ‘incredible honour’ as fans race to buy them
News.content:
Emma Bunton calls limited edition Spice Girls coins an ‘incredible honour’ as fans race to buy them

The Royal Mint has officially launched limited edition Spice Girls coins to time with International Women’s Month, and Emma Bunton has already hailed them an ‘incredible honour’

View 3 Images The Spice Girls have a new limited edition coin collection (Image: Royal Mint)

On Sunday we celebrated International Women’s Day, with March as a whole marking the official International Women’s Month. To mark the occasion, The Royal Mint has launched a brand new collection of Spice Girls coins to celebrate the original “girl power”, making them the first ever female group to have their own coin.

The brand new collection of Spice Girls coins start from £18.50 and go up to £10,535 depending on which type of coin you buy and the rarity of each one. Announcing the launch, Emma Bunton posted on Instagram saying: “How’s this for Girl Power?! The first female group ever to have our very own coin! It’s such an incredible honour to be celebrated by The Royal Mint. What a moment.”





The Spice Girls coins are also perfectly timed to celebrate 30 years of the Spice Girls, who launched their debut chart-topping single Wannabe in 1996. The coins were created in collaboration with the girl group themselves, and come in silver, gold and colour editions.

View 3 Images The Royal Mint has released its new Spice Girls coins (Image: Royal Mint)

The Spice Girls 2026 UK £5 Brilliant Uncirculated Coin is the most affordable piece in the collection, costing £18.50 and is a simple silver coin with the Spice Girls logo on. You can also pick up uncirculated colour editions that pay tribute to Baby, Ginger, Posh, Sporty and Scary Spice, with individual slipcases on each Spice Girl.

There’s only 15,000 of these being launched, with each one costing £29.50. They come with an exclusive slipcase that pays homage to each of the five Spice Girls and their personality depending on which one you choose.

View 3 Images The coins come with collectable sleeves (Image: Royal Mint )

You can also snap up plenty more Spice Girls merchandise to celebrate 30 years since the groups debut. At ASOS, the ASOS Design Relaxed Hoodie With Spice Girls Print is on sale for £25.50 down from £36, whilst the ASOS DESIGN Oversized Sweatshirt With Spice Girls Print is now £27.20 down from £34.

Meanwhile the Official Spice Girls Album T-Shirt is £18.69 down from £19.99 at Amazon, and features the classic Spice Girls logo with each of the members on. It comes in both men’s and women’s sizes, as well as plus size and big and tall sizing options.

Article continues below

However the Royal Mint’s Spice Girls coins are a great limited edition item that collectors and fans of the group will no doubt be racing to get their hands on.

News.title: Liverpool's LIPA School in 'bomb threat' as parents sent worst-nightmare letter
News.content:
Parents have received a disturbing letter from a school with police at the scene over a "bomb threat".

Police rushed to LIPA School in Liverpool this morning after a "threat" was made. In a letter, parents were informed the school had been forced to close for the day due to a threat to cause harm.

The letter explained how these are often hoaxes, and Merseyside Police has also said it believes the threats to be a hoax. The school is working with police, with officers seen inside the school this morning.

Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

Image: Liverpool Echo) Liverpool Echo)

This morning's threat comes after news the school would be closing permanently this year after the government agreed with the school's trustees that it should be shut down.

LIPA School headteacher Claire McKendrick said in her letter to parents: "I am writing to inform you that we have made the unfortunate decision to close the school today. This morning, the school received a bomb threat and a threat to cause harm.

"Whilst these types of messages are, in most cases, hoaxes, the heightened media attention surrounding the school in recent days means we must treat this as a serious and credible risk.

"We are currently working closely with Merseyside Police, who are supporting us in ensuring the safety of our pupils, staff, and wider community.

"I appreciate that this will cause disruption and concern, and I sincerely apologise for that. However, the safety and wellbeing of our pupils, families and staff must always come first.

"We advise families not to enter the school building until we are able to confirm that it is safe to return. We will email parents regarding the reopening of the school as soon as it is safe to do so. Thank you for your understanding, patience, and support."

The school shares a name with Paul McCartney's arts institute but is run by a separate trust. Last year the school received a damning Ofsted report which plunged it into special measures, before safety concerns saw the school close days before the end of the summer term, reported the Liverpool Echo.

Last month it was confirmed that the school will close down this summer, with a letter to parents detailing a host of major issues leading to the decision. The factors detailed include a "significant overspend over a number of years, which has led to a substantial historical financial deficit."

Last year's inadequate Ofsted rating is also mentioned, as is a "significant" fall in pupil numbers as well as "inappropriate" educational facilities and the lack of any viable alternatives.

News.title: Heartbreak for family as mum-of-three dies by suicide after 'difficult period'
News.content:
Heartbreak for family as mum-of-three dies by suicide after 'difficult period'

Michelle Fisher, 42, has been fondly remembered after she died by suicide last week, with friends and her local community in Staffordshire rallying around her grieving family

View Image Michelle Fisher

The family of a beloved and "kind" mum-of-three has been left in shock after she tragically took her own life following a "difficult period".

‌



Michelle Fisher, who had been a teaching assistant at West Hill Primary School in Cannock for around a decade, was a well-recognised and friendly presence within the local community. Sadly, she died by suicide on Friday, February 20, leaving her three children - Morgan, 22, Jake, 18, and Harvey, 13 - devastated and "trying to process the shock".

‌



In a heartfelt tribute from her loved ones, the 42-year-old has been remembered as a "kind" woman who "had a natural ability to make people feel cared for".

‌



The 42-year-old has been remembered as 'kind' in a heartfelt tribute from her loved ones. They said: "Michelle was the kind of person who brought warmth into every room she walked into", reports Birmingham Live. "She had a natural ability to make people feel cared for whether it was through her kindness, her humour, or the way she always put others before herself.

"She was a devoted mum-of-three, and everything she did revolved around making sure her children felt supported, loved, and encouraged in everything they pursued.

"Losing her has left a huge hole in our lives. As a family we are still trying to process the shock, and everyday moments feel completely different now. Her absence is felt constantly, in the quiet moments, in family routines, and in all the little things she used to do without thinking."

‌



They added: "Michelle worked as a teaching assistant at West Hill Primary School for almost 10 years, and during that time she became a familiar and much-loved figure in the local community.

"She had a natural warmth that made children feel comfortable and supported, and many parents spoke highly of the care she showed. She absolutely adored her Labrador, Luna, who was always close by and a big part of her daily life. Above everything, Michelle was devoted to her son's football team, always on the sidelines at their matches, encouraging them every step of the way.

"She loved being involved, often helping out by serving tea and coffee to other supporters. Saturday afternoons were one of her favourite times of the week, spent at Hednesford Town FC watching 'The Pitmen' and enjoying the atmosphere with friends and family. She created a life filled with love, loyalty and community."

‌



In honour of Michelle, Hednesford Town FC will be observing a minute's applause before their match on Saturday, March 14. A GoFundMe page has been established by her loved ones to gather funds for her funeral.

Michelle's family added: "This fundraiser will help us give Michelle a dignified and heartfelt farewell,something that truly reflects the person she was: loving, generous, and full of kindness. It takes away the added stress of financial worry at a time when everything already feels overwhelming.

"Beyond the funeral, the support will help her family, as they navigate life without her. It gives them a sense of security and breathing space while they try to adjust to such an unimaginable loss."

Article continues below

For emotional support, you can call the Samaritans 24-hour helpline on 116 123, email jo@samaritans.org, visit a Samaritans branch in person or go to the Samaritans website.

News.title: Iran war 'could send fuel prices soaring to 180p' as mortgage costs begin to rise
News.content:
Iran war 'could send fuel prices soaring to 180p' as mortgage costs begin to rise

The turmoil caused by the Middle East conflicted has extended into a new week, as Chancellor Rachel Reeves is set to attend emergency meeting

View 4 Images Pain at the pump as fuel prices jump over the weekend (Image: GETTY)

Pump prices broke the 150p a litre barrier and mortgage costs reached almost 5% as the impact of the Middle East war on ordinary UK households deepened.

‌



The ongoing conflict also saw nearly £30billion wiped off UK share prices amid a global stock market rout on Monday.

‌



With inflation expected to be pushed back up, a Bank of England rate cut next week now seems to have evaporated, with even talk of a potential increase in borrowing costs at some stage.

‌



The most immediate fall-out has been from a surge in oil prices, which spiked to a four-year high of nearly $119 a barrel in early trading on Monday.

Hopes that oil reserves could be released saw it ease back, but it was still at more than $100 by mid-morning. A sharp rise in the cost of oil since the US and Israel launched missile attacks on Iran is also feeding through to drivers on the forecourt.

View 4 Images AA advises drivers to cut back on non essential journeys to save on fuel (Image: Maureen McLean/Shutterstock)

‌



The RAC said diesel went from an average of 148.35p per litre on Friday to 150.97p on Sunday - and warned it could keep rising to reach almost 180p. The average for unleaded has also continued to rise, reaching 137.51p a litre on Sunday.

Unleaded has now jumped by 4.68p since February 28, with diesel up even more, soaring by 8.59p a litre - or 6% - over the same period.

RAC head of policy Simon Williams said: “Average petrol and diesel prices have rocketed in the last week and are unfortunately likely to keep on rising, so the situation for UK drivers is looking increasingly bleak. Petrol is up 5p to 137.5p and diesel up 9p to 151p a litre since the current crisis began on Saturday 28 February.

‌



“Unleaded is almost certainly going to reach an average of 140p in the next week or so while diesel looks highly likely to climb to at least 160p a litre.

“The price of diesel is increasing more quickly now than at any point since the start of the Ukraine conflict. With oil at a sustained $100, petrol could rise towards 150p a litre - a price not seen since June 2024. Diesel could reach almost 180p, which would be a three-year high.

“We encourage drivers to continue filling up as normal but to shop around for the best prices using apps like myRAC as there can be big local differences between forecourts. Driving fuel efficiently by avoiding harsh accelerating and braking and ensuring tyres are inflated to the right pressures can help eke out every last mile and save money.”

‌



Edmund King, AA president, urged drivers to consider changing their habits to save on fuel. He said: “As we predicted last week, the longer this conflict goes on, the more effect it will have on the cost of oil.

“Anytime Brent Crude passes $100 per barrel raises concern across the markets, for the haulage industry and drivers. One positive is that there are still reserves of oil out there and IEA intervention may release more supplies.

“In the meantime, there will be gradual increases in pump prices, but this shouldn’t happen overnight as fuel has been purchased at previous prices.

‌



“Our suggestion is that drivers should not change their refuelling habits but can consider cutting out some non-essential journeys and changing their driving style to conserve fuel. These measures linked to warmer weather means than drivers can ensure their fuel stretches further.

“There is still a wide disparity of fuel prices at the pumps so drivers can use the AA App or Government’s fuel finder to find the best pump prices close-by.”

Fixed rate mortgage cost have risen sharply amid the fall-out from the Middle East conflict. Industry experts Moneyfacts says the average two-year fixed residential mortgage rate now stands at 4.87%, up from 4.84% on Friday.

‌



Meanwhile, the average five-year fixed residential mortgage rate today is 4.98%, up from 4.96%. The cost of fixed rate mortgages is determined by what are called swap rates, or how much lenders pay to institutions in return for fixed funding. Those swap rates have jumped on the back of the conflict.

View 4 Images Chancellor Rachel Reeves to meet other G7 finance leaders to discuss fall-out from Middle East crisis (Image: Getty Images)

Riz Malik, independent financial adviser at R3 Wealth, said Donald Trump was “damaging the UK economy”. He added: “The spike in oil price is disastrous for global economies, not just the UK’s.

‌



“Even oil producers don’t want to see oil north of $100 as they know the demand destruction impact it has. Unless this conflict comes to a quick conclusion, inflation will spike and interest rate increases will start getting priced in. Donald Trump is damaging the UK economy and is going to cost UK households money.”

It comes as the war spreads further across the Middle East, with Iran targeting regional energy infrastructure and blocking the crucial Strait of Hormuz shipping route, through which a fifth of the world’s oil is carried.

Chancellor Rachel Reeves is reportedly set to join an emergency meeting of G7 finance ministers later on Monday to discuss a possible joint release of petroleum from reserves co-ordinated by the International Energy Agency to help tackle the surge in oil prices.

‌



Oil prices are now around 60% higher than they were when the war started. By mid-morning, the FTSE 100 was down 120 points at 10,164, as a sell-off in Asia extended into Europe.

About £770billion has been slashed from stock markets around the world since Sunday as the Middle East crisis triggered renewed panic among traders and investors.

The drop threatens to hit the value of pension pots the longer it goes on, as well as those with stocks and shares ISAs and other investments linked to equities. While markets have taken a hit since the war erupted, it comes after a long run which has seen some - including the Footsie - reach record highs.

‌



There concerns the impact from the Middle East war could drive up inflation again and dent already fragile economic growth.

The UK could be among those hardest hit, with the economy already stuck in the slow lane. Interviewed by the Financial Times on Friday, Qatar’s energy minister Saad al-Kaabi warned the war could “bring down the economies of the world” and predicted Gulf energy exporters would stop production within days.

View 4 Images US President Donald Trump accused of "damaging" the UK economy by going to war in the Middle East (Image: AFP via Getty Images)

‌



Richard Hunter, head of markets at the platform Interactive Investor, said: “The mood is darkening among investors as the possibility of an extended conflict increases.

“With the Strait of Hormuz effectively closed for traffic, and with further cuts to production coming from Kuwait and Iraq, the supply shock is truly reverberating and, if left unchecked, will likely lead to a global economic slowdown. The possibility of stagflation – a toxic mix of slowing growth and rising inflation – or even recession are currently on the minds of increasingly concerned investors.”

Susannah Streeter, chief investment atrategist at investment service Wealth Club, said: “Panic has hit equity markets after oil prices rocketed as fears materialised about a big squeeze in global supplies.”

‌



Chris Beauchamp, chief market analyst at online trading and investing platform IG, said: “Investors have woken up to an oil crisis. It may have taken a week, but oil prices have finally crossed the $100 mark that everyone expected would happen if Hormuz was closed.”

The war could leave consumers and businesses worldwide facing weeks or months of higher fuel prices even if the week-old conflict ends quickly, as suppliers grapple with damaged facilities, disrupted logistics and elevated risks to shipping.

Iraqi oil production from its main southern oilfields has fallen by 70% to just 1.3 million barrels per day as the country is unable to export oil via the Strait of Hormuz due to the Iran war. The Kuwait Petroleum Corporation began cutting oil output on Saturday and declared force majeure on shipments, though it did not say how much production it would shut.

Article continues below

As oil prices surged, US Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer called on President Trump to release oil from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve. “President Trump should release oil from the SPR now to stabilize markets, bring prices down, and stop the price shock that American families are already feeling thanks to his reckless war,” Schumer said in a statement.

News.title:
Iran Supreme Leader LIVE: Mojtaba Khamenei signs missile with
message to Israel
News.content:
11:04 Ethan Blackshaw

Starmer hits back at Trump comments on iran

The Prime Minister defended his response to the crisis after repeated criticism from US President Donald Trump.

Sir Keir Starmer said: “It’s really important to emphasise that the US and the UK are working together every single day, as they always have.
